metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = attractive potential (phase advanced, wave pulled in); CRIMSON = repulsive potential (phase retarded, wave pushed out)
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no partial-wave-sum formula, no cross-section derivation, no Levinson theorem

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Fire a beam of particles at a hidden object. You can't see the object. But the wave that comes back has shifted. Its crests land in a different place than they would if nothing were there. That shift is the signature of the force.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Fire a beam of particles at a hidden object. You can't see the object. But the wave that comes back has shifted. Its crests land in a

beats.narration_text: Two scattering experiments. One object pulls the particles toward it. The other pushes them away. You can tell which is which just by watching where the wave crests land — no other information needed.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two scattering experiments. One object pulls the particles toward it. The other pushes them away. You can tell which is which just by watching where the wave cr

beats.narration_text: A single number per force — the phase shift — encodes attractive versus repulsive, weak versus strong. How can one number carry the entire signature of a force you cannot see directly?

props.headline: A single number per force — the phase shift — encodes…

card.headline: Attractive pulls the wave in. Repulsive pushes it out. One number tells the difference.
card.dek: How does a single phase shift encode everything about a hidden force?

beats.narration_text: In quantum mechanics, a scattered particle is a wave. Far from the scatterer, the wave looks like a free particle wave — a simple sine — except it has shifted in phase. That shift is measured by comparing the scattered wave to a reference wave with no scatterer present.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In quantum mechanics, a scattered particle is a wave. Far from the scatterer, the wave looks like a free particle wave — a simple sine — except it has shifted i

beats.narration_text: The reference: a free particle moving past empty space, no force present. Its crests march out at regular spacing. The scattered wave, emerging from a region with a potential, has the same spacing — same wavelength, same energy — but displaced. The distance of that displacement, in wave-cycle units, is the phase shift delta.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The reference: a free particle moving past empty space, no force present. Its crests march out at regular spacing. The scattered wave, emerging from a region wi

beats.narration_text: The phase shift is real and can be positive or negative. A positive delta means the crests are ahead of where they would be for a free particle. A negative delta means they are behind. That sign is precisely what distinguishes an attractive force from a repulsive one.

props.headline: The phase shift is real and can be positive or negative.…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The phase shift is real and can be positive or negative. A positive delta means the crests are ahead of where they would be for a free particle. A negative delt

beats.narration_text: An attractive potential draws the particle inward. Inside the well, the particle moves faster — it gains kinetic energy. Faster motion means shorter wavelength in the interaction region. More wave cycles crammed into the same space. When the particle exits, its crests are ahead of the reference. Phase shift: positive.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: An attractive potential draws the particle inward. Inside the well, the particle moves faster — it gains kinetic energy. Faster motion means shorter wavelength

beats.narration_text: A repulsive potential pushes the particle out. Inside the barrier, the particle slows — it loses kinetic energy. Slower motion means longer wavelength. Fewer wave cycles in the same space. When the particle exits, its crests are behind the reference. Phase shift: negative.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A repulsive potential pushes the particle out. Inside the barrier, the particle slows — it loses kinetic energy. Slower motion means longer wavelength. Fewer wa

beats.narration_text: A strong attractive well pulls the wave in sharply — large positive phase shift. A weak attractive well barely nudges it — small positive phase shift. A deep repulsive barrier pushes it far out — large negative phase shift. The size of delta measures the strength; its sign names the type.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A strong attractive well pulls the wave in sharply — large positive phase shift. A weak attractive well barely nudges it — small positive phase shift. A deep re

beats.narration_text: There is one special value: delta equals plus or minus ninety degrees. At that point the scattered and free waves are perfectly out of step. The scattering is maximally strong — the wave function inside the potential has built up as much amplitude as unitarity allows. This is a resonance.

props.headline: There is one special value: delta equals plus or minus ninety…

card.headline: delta > 0: attractive (wave pulled in). delta < 0: repulsive (wave pushed out). |delta| = 90 deg: resonance.
card.dek: The phase shift is the wave's memory of the force it passed through.

beats.narration_text: Every elastic scattering experiment reduces to determining phase shifts — one number per angular-momentum channel. Measure the scattered intensity at different angles, extract the phase shifts, and you have reconstructed the effective force. No need to see inside the scatterer.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Every elastic scattering experiment reduces to determining phase shifts — one number per angular-momentum channel. Measure the scattered intensity at different

beats.narration_text: In nuclear physics, phase shifts measured from proton-proton scattering at different energies mapped out the nuclear force before any theory could compute it. The wave crests told experimenters what no microscope could see: the shape and sign of the force between nucleons.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In nuclear physics, phase shifts measured from proton-proton scattering at different energies mapped out the nuclear force before any theory could compute it. T

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a spherical potential well, depth two electron-volts, radius half a nanometer. A slow electron approaches — say, kinetic energy one electron-volt. Inside the well the electron moves faster; its wavelength inside is shorter. Compared to a free electron, the exiting wave has advanced by about thirty degrees. Phase shift: positive thirty degrees, attractive well confirmed. Now flip the sign of the potential to a barrier of the same size. The electron slows inside; its crests fall behind by thirty degrees. Phase shift: negative thirty degrees, repulsive barrier confirmed. Same measurement, opposite signs, two different forces identified.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Illustrative: a spherical potential well, depth two electron-volts, radius half a nanometer. A slow electron approaches — say, kinetic energy one electron-volt.

beats.narration_text: The question: how can a single number carry the signature of a hidden force? Because that number is a phase shift — the advance or delay of a wave's crests after passing through the force region. Positive phase shift: attractive, wave pulled in, crests ahead. Negative: repulsive, wave pushed out, crests behind. One number. The whole story. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: The question: how can a single number carry the signature of…

card.headline: delta > 0: attractive. delta < 0: repulsive. One phase shift, one hidden force.
card.dek: The wave remembers what it passed through. Its crests tell you the sign and size of the force.
